channel.title: A Field Guide to Good Arguments on Academis Demo
channel.description: Recent content in A Field Guide to Good Arguments on Academis Demo

item.title: Chapter 1: The Anatomy of an Argument
item.description: The three-part structure An argument is not a fight. In the sense used throughout this guide, an argument is a structured attempt to justify a claim — to give someone good reason to believe something on the basis of evidence and reasoning rather than authority, repetition, or social pressure. Every argument has three structural components: The claim is the position you are defending. It is the answer to the question: what are you asserting?

item.title: Chapter 2: Evidence and Its Limits
item.description: The selection problem Every piece of evidence you present is a selection from a larger set of possible observations. The argument you hear is shaped as much by what was left out as by what was included — and the same is true of arguments you yourself construct. This is not an accusation. Selection is unavoidable in any argument of finite length. The question is whether the selection is made honestly, with an awareness of what the excluded evidence would do to the conclusion.

item.title: Chapter 3: Fallacies and Misdirection
item.description: Why fallacies persist Fallacious arguments are not confined to bad faith or low intelligence. They persist because they work — at least in the short term, at least with audiences who are not actively looking for them. Many fallacies exploit genuine cognitive shortcuts that are adaptive in other contexts. The pattern-matching that makes ad hominem feel like good reasoning is the same pattern-matching that correctly tells us to distrust someone who has lied to us before.

item.title: Chapter 4: Counter-Arguments and Steelmanning
item.description: What steelmanning is Steelmanning is the practice of identifying and engaging with the strongest version of an opposing argument — as opposed to the weakest version, which is what strawmanning addresses. It is the opposite of the tendency to represent an opponent’s position in the most easily defeated form. The term is not universally used, and the concept is simple enough not to require it. The question is always: if a thoughtful, well-informed person held the opposite view, what would their best argument be?

item.title: Chapter 5: Writing the Argument
item.description: Why writing changes the argument Before you write an argument, you have a mental sketch — a rough sense of the claim, a cluster of supporting ideas, some intuition about the objections. This sketch feels more complete than it is, because your mind fills in the gaps automatically as you think. Writing forces the gaps into the open. The sentence that refuses to complete itself, the paragraph that keeps doubling back, the transition that sounds reasonable out loud but reads as a non sequitur on the page — these are not stylistic failures.
